content: VERL (Volcano Engine RL) is a distributed RL training framework developed by ByteDance for large-scale LLM training. It natively supports GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) and is optimized for multi-node distributed training with Ray/FSDP integration. It targets GPU clusters with tens to hundreds of GPUs. trl (Transformer Reinforcement Learning) is a HuggingFace library that applies reinforcement learning to LLMs, providing PPO, DPO, and SFT algorithms with seamless integration into the transformers/peft ecosystem. trl works from single GPU to multi-GPU setups. For the MED project, VERL is the primary choice for GRPO+TinyLoRA distributed training, while trl serves as an alternative for PPO/DPO/SFT on smaller setups.
title: VERL vs trl: RL frameworks for LLM training

content: GPU/CPU/APU compute backends for LLM inference and training: CUDA (NVIDIA, de facto standard for training and inference), ROCm (AMD discrete GPUs, PyTorch official support), oneAPI (Intel GPUs, developing). For inference-only: Vulkan (cross-vendor, works on APUs), OpenCL (legacy cross-vendor), Metal (Apple Silicon). For CPU: AVX2/AVX-512 SIMD instructions used by llama.cpp, OpenBLAS/MKL for linear algebra. Key framework compatibility: llama.cpp supports CUDA/ROCm/Vulkan/CPU, Ollama (llama.cpp-based) same coverage, vLLM supports CUDA/ROCm only, PyTorch supports CUDA/ROCm/CPU. Training frameworks: trl supports CUDA/ROCm/CPU, VERL primarily CUDA only.
title: Compute backends for LLM: CUDA, ROCm, Vulkan, CPU

content: AMD APU (integrated GPU like Radeon 680M/780M/890M) is NOT supported by ROCm. ROCm only supports datacenter GPUs (MI250, MI300X) and discrete GPUs (RX 7900 XTX, RX 7900 XT, RX 7800 XT). On APU systems, GPU workloads fall back to CPU execution. However, Vulkan works on APUs, so llama.cpp and Ollama can leverage APU GPU cores for inference via Vulkan backend. For training, APU systems are limited to CPU-based training with trl, which is slow but feasible for TinyLoRA (13 parameters only). Best practice for APU + high RAM: use Ollama with Vulkan for inference, cloud GPU for training.
title: AMD APU limitations: ROCm unsupported, Vulkan for inference

content: LLM quantization quality degradation by bit level: Q8 (~0.5% loss, practically lossless), Q6_K (~1% loss), Q5_K_M (~1.5% loss), Q4_K_M (~2-3% loss, practical standard), Q3_K_M (~5-8% loss, visible in long text), Q2_K (~10-20% loss, clear degradation), IQ1_M (~25-40% loss, experimental). Key degradation patterns at Q2: low-frequency knowledge becomes inaccurate, long reasoning chains break down, code generation edge-case errors increase. Classification tasks (like difficulty tagging) are more robust to quantization than generation tasks (like seed document creation).
title: Quantization quality degradation: Q8 to Q1 empirical analysis

content: Research on quantization robustness and weight redundancy in LLMs: (1) Large models are more resilient to quantization due to parameter redundancy - confirmed by Red Hat's 500K+ evaluations (70B/405B show negligible degradation). (2) 'Low-Bit Quantization Favors Undertrained LLMs' (ACL 2025) shows undertrained models are robust to quantization because weight fluctuations make them insensitive to small perturbations, while fully trained models have less redundancy and are more sensitive. (3) LeanQuant (ICLR 2025) found 0.1-1% of weights are 'outliers' with activation scales 100x larger than normal - these are critical and get destroyed at Q2. (4) OneBit (NeurIPS 2024) achieved 81% of original performance with 1-bit weights, decomposing weight matrices into sign matrix + value vectors, suggesting information is primarily in weight direction, not magnitude.
title: Research: weight redundancy protects against quantization degradation

content: Mixed-precision quantization strategies for LLMs: 'Protecting the Edges' keeps embedding layers, first/last transformer blocks, and output head at FP16/BF16 while compressing middle layers to low-bit. Layer-Wise Quantization (2024) measures layer importance via output embedding change and outlier weight density. MixLLM (Microsoft, 2024) applies mixed precision at output-feature level within the same layer. LUQ (2025) uses activation entropy to identify redundant layers for ultra-low-bit quantization. LLM-MQ uses gradient-based sensitivity for automatic bit-width allocation under memory budget constraints.
title: Mixed-precision quantization: Protecting the Edges, MixLLM, LUQ

content: GGUF quantization naming convention: Q{bits}_{method}_{size}. Method: K=K-Quant (new, recommended), no suffix=Legacy. Size: S=Small (no protection), M=Medium (important layers +2 bits), L=Large (max protection). Examples: Q4_K_M means middle layers 4-bit, important layers 6-bit. Q3_K_L means middle layers 3-bit, important layers 5-bit. 'Important layers' include attention output projections, FFN output projections, first/last transformer blocks, embedding/lm_head. IQ series (IQ2_XS, IQ3_M, IQ4_XS etc.) uses imatrix (importance matrix) calibration for data-driven precision allocation per weight group, rather than rule-based layer assignment. imatrix effect is strongest at low bit widths (Q2-Q3) where optimal bit allocation matters most.
title: GGUF quantization types: K-Quant, IQ, imatrix explained

content: imatrix (Importance Matrix) quantization works in 3 steps: (1) Run calibration data (hundreds to thousands of text samples) through the model. (2) Record importance score for each weight: importance[i][j] = sum of (activation[i] * weight[i][j])^2. Frequently activated, high-value weights score higher. (3) Allocate quantization precision based on importance - high-importance weight groups get more bits, low-importance groups get compressed more. Unlike K-Quant which assigns precision by layer type (rule-based, no data needed), imatrix assigns precision per weight group within each layer (data-driven, finer granularity). imatrix has minimal effect at Q4+ but significant improvement at Q3 and below.
title: imatrix quantization: data-driven precision allocation

content: Teacher model selection for MED memory maturation with 128GB RAM: Recommended models in 40-120B range: (1) Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct Q8 (~75GB) - best Student/Teacher alignment with Qwen-7B Student, strong Japanese support. (2) Qwen2.5-Coder-32B Q8 (~34GB) - code-specialized, best for code quality review. (3) Llama3.1-70B Q8 (~73GB) - strongest general English reasoning. (4) gpt-oss-120B Q4 (~67GB) - GPT-4o equivalent open weights. Key principle: 72B Q8 > 120B Q4 ≈ 236B Q2. Higher quantization quality on smaller model often beats lower quality on larger model. For dual-teacher setup: gpt-oss-120B Q4 (67GB) + Qwen2.5-72B IQ4_XS (40GB) = 107GB, fits in 128GB.
title: Teacher model selection: 72B Q8 vs 120B Q4 for 128GB RAM

content: TinyLoRA (Morris et al., 2026) achieves 91% on GSM8K with only 13 trainable parameters. The key insight is that knowledge already exists in the model (or external memory like FAISS), and only the 'skill of using knowledge' needs to be taught via RL. In MED, knowledge is stored in FAISS external memory, and TinyLoRA teaches the Student model retrieval and utilization skills. Configuration: frozen_rank=2, projection_dim=4, tie_factor=7. The adapter weights are approximately 1KB each, making cloud-to-local transfer trivial. Training can be done on cloud GPU (VERL+CUDA) and the tiny adapter downloaded to local APU environment for inference.
title: TinyLoRA: 13 parameters, knowledge-use skill via RL

content: For MED system running on AMD APU with 128GB RAM, the recommended architecture is: Local (APU): Orchestrator + FAISS + GUI + Student inference via Ollama/Vulkan. Teacher inference via LM Studio on separate 128GB machine. Cloud (GPU): GRPO + TinyLoRA training only (adapter ~1KB download). This hybrid approach maximizes local capability while minimizing cloud cost. With 128GB RAM, models up to 72B Q8 or even 236B Q2 can run, but 72B Q8 quality exceeds 236B Q2 due to quantization degradation. Ollama with Vulkan backend can leverage APU GPU cores for inference acceleration.
title: MED hybrid architecture: local APU inference + cloud GPU training
